note: Whisper base-model source had Swiss-German recognition errors; English is intent-faithful.

segments.text: Hi everyone, I'm Stefan from the Flugschule Frewings flight school, and today I want to talk a bit about aerodynamics.
Aerodynamics in general, but above all paraglider-specific.
The question that surely concerns all of us — how does a paraglider actually fly?
Ever since the first moment as a kid, when I saw a paraglider launch, I couldn't let go of it — I've always been fascinated.
How can it be that a wing made from a bit of fabric and a few lines, pulled out of a backpack,
lifts us into the air — how can you fly with this thing?
And once you dig into the basics, you quickly find out that a paraglider works exactly the same way.
Like an aeroplane, like a bird too — we have a wing surface that generates lift, and through that produces a force
that keeps us up in the air.
I'll compare it a bit to a car.
Everyone can drive a car, but if you really understand how the clutch and the engine and everything works in detail,
With the paraglider it can also happen that you're a super top pilot and don't really know
what's actually going on above you.
But I'm of the opinion that this is relatively important for paragliding.
Maybe more so than for driving a car.
So that you understand the technical background a little bit,
because it matters for safety, and also when you want to get the full performance out of the wing,
when later on you fly a bit more ambitiously and things get serious.
